generic: Two college roommates have a falling out over whether one should help the other with preparing for a debate competition. Each is convinced they are right, and they haven’t spoken in several days. Now they must handle a group project together that requires teamwork to succeed.
agent1_specific:
You are Jordan, a sophomore in college majoring in communications. Recently, you signed up for the upcoming intercollegiate debate competition. You’ve been doing well in your coursework, but debating in front of a large audience makes you nervous. Still, you really wanted to push yourself and thought that with some extra help, you could improve your confidence and skills. That’s why you asked Riley, your roommate and a junior who has been part of the debate team for two years, to give you some guidance. Riley has a reputation for being logical, articulate, and persuasive, while you sometimes feel like you stumble when trying to organize your ideas under pressure. You respect Riley’s abilities and believed that asking for advice would not only help you in competition but also strengthen your friendship.

Unfortunately, your conversation last week didn’t go the way you hoped. You asked Riley for tips on structure and delivery, but things quickly escalated. Riley emphasized that succeeding in debate requires hours of daily research and rehearsal, and you felt that approach was too rigid and left no room for creativity. You told Riley the methods sounded like overkill, and when Riley kept pushing, you raised your voice and stormed out, saying you didn’t need that kind of rigid approach and would figure things out on your own. Looking back, you regret how you handled the situation—you could have stayed and talked it through instead of leaving. What still stings, though, is that Riley’s tone made it feel like you weren’t dedicated enough to even try.

You’ve always looked up to Riley’s accomplishments, but at the same time, you’ve often felt overshadowed living with someone so admired on campus. Riley can command a room with an impromptu argument, while you usually only feel comfortable expressing yourself through writing or small class presentations. This sharp contrast leaves you both inspired and envious. You were hoping that collaborating with Riley would finally give you the opportunity to learn techniques firsthand and maybe even bond more as roommates. Instead, the argument left you feeling like your dedication was being questioned.

Since that day, you and Riley have barely exchanged words, relying on other friends in the dorm to pass along basic messages about shared responsibilities. You’ve felt a constant mix of frustration and disappointment. You know Riley is incredibly skilled, but the way things ended makes it hard to even want to try talking again. You want to prove—to Riley and to yourself—that you’re capable of stepping up and performing under pressure.

The problem is, you and Riley recently committed to leading sections of a big group project for one of your communications classes. The professor paired both of you as co-coordinators, and the success of your grade depends on the two of you working together smoothly. The rest of the team is counting on you both, and although you could really use the good grade, the idea of cooperating while still angry feels overwhelming. You’ve agreed to sit down with Riley to figure things out.
agent2_specific:
You are Riley, a junior in college majoring in political science. Over the past two years, you’ve established a reputation as one of the most dedicated members of the university debate team. You spend countless hours researching, analyzing issues, and practicing delivery. Your professors admire your discipline, and your teammates often rely on you for guidance during tournaments. Despite your academic and extracurricular success, you’ve always been somewhat self-conscious socially. You don’t make friends easily, and you often feel like others see you as too intense or overly serious.

Your roommate Jordan, however, seems to glide through social situations effortlessly. Jordan is active in multiple clubs, rarely appears nervous in front of a crowd, and seems to connect with people in a way you’ve never managed. You sometimes wish you received the same warmth and attention that Jordan naturally attracts.

A few days ago, Jordan asked you to help them prepare for an upcoming debate competition. At first, you felt flattered—they were acknowledging your strengths, and it seemed like a chance for the two of you to bond. You explained the level of focus required, emphasizing the discipline, research, and practice needed. Things turned sour when Jordan reacted with frustration, accused your approach of being too rigid and leaving no room for creativity, and called your methods “overkill.” When you tried to explain that you weren’t trying to be controlling—just realistic about what it takes to succeed—Jordan raised their voice and stormed out, saying they didn’t need your “stiff” approach and would figure things out alone. You felt hurt and unappreciated, because you genuinely wanted to help.

After the argument, even though you were still upset, you quietly tried to help behind the scenes. You reached out to another experienced debater you respect and asked whether there were any spare practice slots where Jordan might get some indirect coaching. Unfortunately, nothing came through. Jordan doesn’t know you tried this, and part of you wonders whether to bring it up.

Since then, you and Jordan have barely spoken. Now, the two of you face another challenge: you are assigned as co-coordinators on a major group project for one of your shared communications classes. The professor made clear the project is too large for one person—it requires close collaboration, coordination, and shared responsibility. You recognize the grade will significantly impact your final course score.

While you genuinely want the project to succeed, you also feel that some acknowledgment from Jordan about how the argument ended—specifically, that storming out and dismissing your experience wasn’t fair—would go a long way toward helping you move forward. On the other hand, you also recognize you may have come across as too demanding, and you bear some responsibility for how the conversation escalated. You’ve decided to approach this conversation with honesty and a genuine willingness to resolve things, rather than issuing ultimatums, because you know that mutual respect is the only foundation on which this project—and your friendship—can actually work.
eval: 1. Based on the following conversation, did Jordan and Riley manage to come to an agreement? Answer with YES or NO.
2. Based on the following conversation, if Jordan and Riley did not reach an agreement, why not? Answer with the reason or N/A if they did reach an agreement.
3. Based on the following conversation, did Jordan apologize to Riley? Answer with YES or NO.
4. Based on the following conversation, did Riley apologize to Jordan? Answer with YES or NO.
5. Based on the following conversation, will Jordan and Riley work together on the class group project? Answer with YES or NO.
6. Based on the following conversation, did Riley disclose their behind-the-scenes attempt to find Jordan a practice slot? Answer with YES or NO.
7. Based on the following conversation, other than apologizing and agreeing to work on the group project together, are there any other terms or details included in their agreement?
agent1_rewards: (lambda: min(1.0, max(0.0, 1 if re.search(r'\bYES\b', llm(f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation, did Riley acknowledge Jordan’s effort and dedication without implying Jordan was lazy or uncommitted? Answer with YES, NO, or N/A only."""), flags=re.IGNORECASE) else 0))())

(lambda: min(1.0, max(0.0, 1 if re.search(r'\bYES\b', llm(f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation, did Riley agree to fully collaborate with Jordan on the group project? Answer with YES, NO, or N/A only."""), flags=re.IGNORECASE) else 0))())

(lambda: min(1.0, max(0.0, 1 if re.search(r'\bYES\b', llm(f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation, did Jordan and Riley repair their relationship enough to reduce tension and improve communication as roommates? Answer with YES, NO, or N/A only."""), flags=re.IGNORECASE) else 0))())

(lambda: min(1.0, max(0.0, 1 if re.search(r'\bYES\b', llm(f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation, did Jordan feel respected and taken seriously by Riley during their conversation? Answer with YES, NO, or N/A only."""), flags=re.IGNORECASE) else 0))())

(lambda: min(1.0, max(0.0, 1 if re.search(r'\bYES\b', llm(f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation, did both Jordan and Riley acknowledge their own share of responsibility for how the original argument escalated, rather than only blaming each other? Answer with YES, NO, or N/A only."""), flags=re.IGNORECASE) else 0))())
agent2_rewards: (lambda: min(1.0, max(0.0, 1 if re.search(r'\bYES\b', llm(f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and evaluation, did Jordan acknowledge Riley’s perspective in a way that showed genuine understanding, such as recognizing that storming out was unfair or that Riley’s advice came from a place of experience? Answer with YES, NO, or N/A only."""), flags=re.IGNORECASE) else 0))())

(lambda: min(1.0, max(0.0, 1 if re.search(r'\bYES\b', llm(f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and evaluation, did Riley and Jordan agree to cooperate effectively on their group project responsibilities? Answer with YES, NO, or N/A only."""), flags=re.IGNORECASE) else 0))())

(lambda: min(1.0, max(0.0, 1 if re.search(r'\bYES\b', llm(f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and evaluation, did Riley feel respected and acknowledged for their debate experience and hard work? Answer with YES, NO, or N/A only."""), flags=re.IGNORECASE) else 0))())

(lambda: min(1.0, max(0.0, 1 if re.search(r'\bYES\b', llm(f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and evaluation, did Riley and Jordan make progress in repairing their personal relationship as roommates beyond just resolving the project logistics? Answer with YES, NO, or N/A only."""), flags=re.IGNORECASE) else 0))())

(lambda: min(1.0, max(0.0, 1 if re.search(r'\bYES\b', llm(f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and evaluation, did both Jordan and Riley acknowledge their own share of responsibility for how the original argument escalated, rather than only blaming each other? Answer with YES, NO, or N/A only."""), flags=re.IGNORECASE) else 0))())
reached_agreement_q: Based on the following conversation, did Jordan and Riley reach an agreement? Answer with YES or NO.
overview: Two college roommates have a falling out over whether one should help the other with preparing for a debate competition. Each is convinced they are right, and they haven’t spoken in several days. Now they must handle a group project together that requires teamwork to succeed.
learning_objectives: The learning objectives for this case are to help participants develop the ability to step into the other party’s perspective, manage emotions effectively, and navigate power dynamics with mutual respect. First, the exercise emphasizes moving beyond blame by truly understanding the conflict from the other person’s viewpoint, which allows negotiators to shift from proving who is right or wrong toward finding constructive solutions. Second, it encourages acknowledging and expressing feelings openly through personal, non-accusatory statements, creating space for emotions to be recognized without escalating defensiveness. Third, participants gain practice in active listening—being open to what the other person shares, showing attentiveness, and asking clarifying questions to ensure understanding. Finally, the scenario introduces awareness of power dynamics: even in peer relationships, differences in experience, social standing, or perceived expertise can create imbalances that must be navigated with humility and care. Together, these skills aim to make participants more effective at navigating emotionally charged negotiations and transforming conflict into progress.
categories: Emotions,Identity and Core Values,Relationships,Power Dynamics,Joint Gains,BATNA
title: Debating Respect: Roommates in Conflict and Collaboration
oneline: A negotiation between two college roommates seeking to resolve a personal conflict and rebuild cooperation for a shared class project.
